notes: Controversial ruling — jury found infringement based on feel not melody. Widely criticized for expanding copyright scope.
En banc 9th Circuit reversed earlier panel. Descending chromatic bassline ruled not protectable. Key case for thin copyright.
Repeated 8-note ostinato ruled unprotectable — too simple and commonplace.

case_name: Bright Tunes Music v. Harrisongs Music
notes: George Harrison found liable for subconscious copying. Intent is not required for infringement.

No proof of access — plaintiff could not show Bee Gees heard his song. Access requirement upheld. Scored: melodic=0.107, harmonic=0.547, rhythmic=0.430, ngram=0.025. Notably low melodic score despite a jury initially finding infringement by ear (verdict later overturned for lack of access evidence, not lack of similarity) — illustrates how by-ear and algorithmic similarity can diverge.

case_name: Three Boys Music Corp. v. Bolton
plaintiff_work: Love Is a Wonderful Thing (Isley Brothers)
notes: Jury inferred access from song's wide distribution 25 years prior. Substantial similarity found on melody.

Foundational case establishing two-part test: copying in fact and improper appropriation. Remanded for trial; Porter prevailed. Scored pairing per Judge Frank opinion: opening 4 notes of Modern Messiah match Don't Fence Me In's 'starry skies above' passage (most concrete comparison cited). Arnstein also alleged Lord Is My Shepherd/Begin the Beguine, A Mother's Prayer/My Heart Belongs to Daddy, I Love You Madly/Night and Day — not scored here. Plaintiff audio source: MCIR (blogs.law.gwu.edu/mcir), a rare case with the obscure original recording archived.
9th Circuit reversed summary judgment — enough similarity to go to jury. Final trial: no infringement. Important for musical element analysis.

case_name: Grand Upright Music v. Warner Bros. Records
notes: Biz Markie sampling case. Judge opened ruling with Thou shalt not steal. Established that sampling requires clearance.
Supreme Court unanimously held parody is fair use even for commercial purposes. Transformative use doctrine.
Beastie Boys sampled 3-note flute passage. Court ruled de minimis. Created circuit split with 6th Circuit on de minimis for samples.

plaintiff_work: Get Off Your Ass and Jam
notes: 6th Circuit: no de minimis defense for sound recording samples. Get a license or do not sample. Directly contradicts 9th Circuit.
Madonna sampled 0.23-second horn hit. 9th Circuit applied de minimis — directly contradicting 6th Circuit Bridgeport rule.

UK High Court granted Sheeran declaration of non-infringement (2022). Chokri ("Sami Switch") alleged Shape of You's 'Oh I, Oh I, Oh I' hook copied from Oh Why's 'Oh why, Oh why, Oh why' refrain. Judge Zacaroli found Sheeran had not heard Oh Why (only 2 radio plays, ~13k YouTube views, small venue performances) and did not deliberately or subconsciously copy it. High harmonic (0.849) and rhythmic (0.402) similarity despite no infringement — access, not similarity, was the deciding factor.

Jury found Morris Albert's 'Feelings' infringed a little-known French composer's earlier song. Rare pre-1990s case where an obscure plaintiff won against a commercially huge hit. Scored: melodic=0.314, harmonic=0.683, rhythmic=0.206, ngram=0.034.

Confirmed 2026-07-03: exact titles are 'Till You' (Repp, 1978, liturgical song based on Luke) vs 'Phantom Song' (Lloyd Webber, mid-1980s, Phantom of the Opera). Complex history — S.D.N.Y. granted summary judgment for Webber (1994), 2nd Cir. reversed and remanded for trial (1997), Webber won at the final jury trial (Dec 1998). Webber's counterclaim that 'Till You' copied 'Close Every Door' (Joseph and the Amazing Technicolor Dreamcoat) was dismissed for no substantial similarity. Net outcome: no infringement. Scored: melodic=0.341, harmonic=0.946, rhythmic=0.400, ngram=0.012. High harmonic similarity but no infringement — consistent with the dataset's pattern that harmony alone rarely decides these cases.

Corrected 2026-07-03 — original draft had wrong songs entirely (Isley Brothers/Chi-Lites titles were unrelated). Actual case: Ruth Stratchborneo's 'Mojo Workout' vs. Preston Foster's 1956 'Got My Mojo Working' (recorded by Muddy Waters, Ann Cole, Jimmy Smith, Bill Cosby). S.D.N.Y. 1973: no infringement — court held 'mojo' was substantially in the public domain and neither music nor words were strikingly similar. Scored: melodic=0.158, harmonic=0.981, rhythmic=0.399, ngram=0.000. Near-maximal harmonic similarity (0.981) yet no infringement — court's reasoning was about public-domain subject matter, not lack of musical similarity, so this is an expected outlier rather than a counterexample.

case_name: Francis, Day & Hunter Ltd v. Bron
notes: Corrected 2026-07-03 — defendant song 'Why' was composed by Peter de Angelis (lyrics Bob Marcucci), 1959, recorded by Frankie Avalon; Sydney Bron/Debmar Publishing was the UK distributor sued, not the songwriter. Plaintiff work 'In a Little Spanish Town' (Mabel Wayne, 1926). UK Court of Appeal: 'definite/considerable' similarity found but no infringement — insufficient evidence of conscious or subconscious copying; similarity held coincidental. Landmark UK case on subconscious copying. Scored: melodic=0.219, harmonic=0.874, rhythmic=0.396, ngram=0.011.

9th Circuit initially revived the suit over the 'players gonna play / haters gonna hate' lyric, then it was dismissed again on remand for lack of originality. Good example of a low-originality lyrics-only claim.

Corrected 2026-07-03 — was mislabeled as a Drake/Lil Wayne dispute. Actual case: New Orleans jazz musician Paul Batiste v. Macklemore & Ryan Lewis (5th Cir. 2020), alleging 'Thrift Shop' copied the beat, drums, intro, bass line, and melody of Batiste's 'Hip Jazz' (one of 11 songs in the original claim; this is the most concretely-alleged pairing). Summary judgment for defendants affirmed — Batiste couldn't show access or striking similarity; he and his attorney were ordered to pay defendants' fees. Scored: melodic=0.178, harmonic=0.536, rhythmic=0.241, ngram=0.000. Low across all metrics, consistent with the courts finding no substantial similarity and no access.

case_name: Sheeran v. Structured Asset Sales / Townsend Estate
notes: SDNY jury found no infringement; centered on whether a common chord progression and harmonic rhythm are protectable. Good companion case to Williams v. Gaye for the Marvin Gaye estate's IP strategy. Scored: melodic=0.254, harmonic=0.910, rhythmic=0.301, ngram=0.018. High harmonic similarity (shared chord progression/harmonic rhythm) but no infringement — the exact fact pattern the case turned on (is a common progression protectable?).

case_name: Estate of Smith v. Cash Money Records
notes: 2nd Cir. affirmed dismissal of organist Jimmy Smith's estate's claim that Drake's track unlawfully sampled a spoken-word intro from 'Jimmy Smith Rap', largely on fair-use grounds. Corrected 2026-06-21 — original draft had wrong song titles for this row.

plaintiff_work: Catalog tracks owned by TufAmerica (exact titles VERIFY)
defendant_work: Paul's Boutique tracks — Beastie Boys (exact titles VERIFY)
notes: VERIFY exact sampled-track titles before searching — TufAmerica sued the Beastie Boys over multiple uncleared samples on 'Paul's Boutique'; SDNY/2nd Cir. dismissed largely on statute-of-limitations/laches grounds rather than reaching the merits of similarity.

Corrected 2026-07-03 — exact titles confirmed. Saregama sued over an unlicensed ~1-second sample (descending G minor arpeggio, 3 notes) from Bollywood song 'Baghon Mein Bahar Hai' used in Timbaland-produced 'Put You On the Game' (The Game, 2005, S.D. Fla./11th Cir.). Dismissed 2009: license was only a 2-year exclusive (expired), and works were not substantially similar. Scored: melodic=0.231, harmonic=0.988, rhythmic=0.247, ngram=0.009.

Corrected 2026-07-03 — was mislabeled as a Dr. Dre instrumental dispute. Actual case: Marlon Williams (Marley Marl) v. Calvin Broadus (Snoop Dogg), S.D.N.Y. 2001 (Judge Mukasey). Defendants conceded 'Ghetto Symphony' used some lyrics/music of 'The Symphony' without permission; court denied summary judgment on whether Williams' own copyright was invalid (his song sampled Otis Redding's 'Hard to Handle'). No published final resolution found — treated as settled/infringement-implied like similar unresolved sampling suits in this dataset. Scored: melodic=0.400, harmonic=0.936, rhythmic=0.000, ngram=0.000. Caveat: rhythmic/ngram=0 may reflect that the specific sampled riff (Otis Redding-derived horn stab) does not fall within the first 90s of either track uploaded for transcription — a known limitation of the trim-to-90s pipeline for deep-cut samples rather than a genuine absence of rhythmic similarity.

defendant_work: Get Dumb! (Free Your Body) / Show Me Love
notes: VERIFY before relying — SDNY denied summary judgment for the sampling defendants, allowing the case to reach a jury on whether a vocal sample was a substantial/recognizable taking; outcome here assumes settlement implied liability, confirm independently.

case_name: Tin Pan Apple, Inc. v. Miller Brewing Co.
notes: VERIFY before relying — SDNY case over an advertiser's unlicensed use of a recognizable Davy DMX beatbox pattern; treat outcome/settled as tentative until confirmed against a primary source.

case_name: Bridgeport Music, Inc. v. Justin Combs Publishing
plaintiff_work: Singing in the Morning (various Bridgeport-owned funk masters)
notes: One of many Bridgeport Music sampling suits arising from its acquisition of George Clinton/P-Funk masters; 6th Circuit affirmed liability for an unlicensed sample. Good rhythm-only-element data point.

case_name: Bridgeport Music, Inc. v. UMG Recordings
notes: Companion Bridgeport sampling suit; court found infringement on at least one sampled track while rejecting others on de minimis grounds — illustrates the per-sample analysis courts use.

case_name: Larrikin Music Publishing v. EMI Songs Australia
plaintiff_work: Kookaburra Sits in the Old Gum Tree
notes: Men at Work's flute riff in 'Down Under' found to reproduce a substantial part of the 1934 campfire song 'Kookaburra'. Non-US case; widely seen as a controversial application of substantial-part doctrine to a brief, simple riff. Scored: melodic=0.230, harmonic=0.791, rhythmic=0.000, ngram=0.009. Caveat: rhythmic=0 likely reflects the contested flute riff occurring outside the first 90s of 'Down Under' (it appears later in the song, not in the intro) rather than a genuine absence of rhythmic similarity.

Confirmed 2026-07-03: Verve had a limited license (via Decca) to sample an orchestral cover of 'The Last Time' but ABKCO/Klein deemed the sampled portion larger than agreed. 1997 settlement: Verve forfeited 100% of royalties and songwriting credit to Jagger-Richards. In 2019, ABKCO/Jagger/Richards voluntarily returned royalties and credit to Ashcroft (Ivor Novello Award announcement). Scored: melodic=0.168, harmonic=0.723, rhythmic=0.324, ngram=0.000.

Huey Lewis sued over similarities between the bassline/groove of 'I Want a New Drug' and the 'Ghostbusters' theme; settled confidentially before trial. Classic groove-vs-melody dispute. Scored: melodic=0.261, harmonic=0.953, rhythmic=0.263, ngram=0.008. Very high harmonic similarity (0.953) for a case explicitly about bassline/groove — the groove similarity likely shows up more in rhythmic/bass features not fully captured by these four metrics.

case_name: Vanilla Ice sample dispute ("Ice Ice Baby")
notes: Queen and David Bowie's publishers were credited and compensated after Vanilla Ice initially denied sampling the bassline of 'Under Pressure'. Good example of an undeniable sample resolved without a published opinion. Scored: melodic=0.195, harmonic=0.960, rhythmic=0.088, ngram=0.011. Very high harmonic similarity (0.960) reflects the undeniable direct bassline sample; low rhythmic score likely reflects added hip-hop drum layers changing the onset pattern despite the shared bass harmony.

Matt Cardle co-writer sued Ed Sheeran over similarities in the chorus melody of 'Photograph'; settled confidentially before trial. Non-US, pairs well with the later Sheeran v. Chokri win for tracking Sheeran's litigation history. Scored: melodic=0.242, harmonic=0.881, rhythmic=0.394, ngram=0.010.

Joe Satriani sued Coldplay over chord-progression and melodic similarity; settled confidentially before trial, terms undisclosed. Useful harmony-driven dispute to contrast with Skidmore's melody/bassline focus. Scored: melodic=0.225, harmonic=0.571, rhythmic=0.359, ngram=0.031.
